國立臺灣科技大學工業管理系  
碩士學位論文 
 
學號：M11201305 
 
神經演化強化學習於倉儲機器人交通控管策略 
Neuroevolutionary Reinforcement Learning for Traffic 
Optimization in Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems 
 
研 究 生：蔡豐興  
碩士 
指導教授：周碩彥 博士 
郭伯勳 博士 
 
中華民國 114 年 6 月 

 
 


 
 


 
I 
論文摘要 
 本研究針對機器人移動履行系統 (RMFS) 中因交通壅塞所引發的「吞吐量-能
源效率」權衡問題，提出一套基於神經演化強化學習 (NERL) 的自適應交通控管
策略。為評估此策略在權衡空間中的定位，本研究於一高擬真度模擬平台，將  
NERL 與代表靜態中心化 （時間基礎） 及去中心化 （基於優先級） 的兩種基線策略，
在標準化的中等密度場景下進行了系統性的比較分析。 
 實驗結果量化了不同策略的性能取捨：去中心化策略雖達到最高的系統吞吐
量，卻也伴隨著最高的能源消耗。相較之下，本研究提出的 NERL 策略在能源效
率上展現顯著優勢，其單均能耗較去中心化策略降低了 19.4% ，較時間基礎策略降
低了 13.1% ，同時持了了與時間基礎策略相的的單單吞吐量。本研究的心心獻在在
於 ， 證實了 NERL 控制器並非在所有指標上超越傳統方法 ， 而是在 「吞吐量-能耗」
的權衡空間中，成功找到一個以能源效率為導向的新穎平衡點，為追求營運成本與
可了續性的智慧倉儲系統，提供了一套有效的替代性優化方案。 
 
關鍵字 ：自動化倉儲機機器人移動履行系統機交通控制機度度強化學習機神經演化機
多目標優化 
  

 
II 
ABSTRACT 
 This study proposes an adaptive traffic control strategy based on Neuroevolution 
Reinforcement Learning (NERL) to address the critical trade -off between throughput 
and energy efficiency caused by traffic congestion in Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems 
(RMFS). To evaluate the positioning of this strategy within the trade -off space, this 
research conducted a systematic comparison of NERL against two baselines representing 
static centralized (Time -Based) and decentralized (Priority -Based) philosophies on a 
high-fidelity simulation platform under a standardized medium-density scenario. 
 The results quantify the distinct performance profiles: the decentralized strategy 
achieves the hig

hest system throughput but at the cost of the highest energy consumption. 
In contrast, the proposed NERL strategy demonstrates significant advantages in ene rgy 
efficiency, reducing the energy consumption per order by 19.4% compared to the 
decentralized strategy and 13.1% compared to the time-based strategy, while maintaining 
a comparable throughput to the time -based approach. The core contribution of this 
research is the empirical validation that the NERL controller successfully identifies a 
novel, energy-efficient operating point within the throughput-energy trade-off space. This 
provides an effective alternative optimization solution for intelligent warehous es 
prioritizing operational costs and sustainability. 
 
Keywords: Automated Warehouse, Robotic Mobile Fulfillment System (RMFS), Traffic 
Control, Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL), Neuroevolution, Multi -objective 
Optimization

. Projections indicate that the annual value of global retail e -commerce will 
grow to $6.42 trillion by 2025, accounting for over 20% of total retail sales [1]. To cop e 
with the new normal of e -commerce orders —characterized by small batches, high 
frequency, and time sensitivity—traditional warehousing models are no longer adequate, 
prompting widespread adoption of Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems (RMFS). In an 
RMFS, hundreds of Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs) work collaboratively within a 
grid-based warehouse, capable of increasing order picking efficiency severalfold. 
 However, high -density AMR operations have given rise to new operational 
bottlenecks. Traffic congestion at intersections, waiting delays between robots, and 
frequent acceleration and deceleration behaviors  not only directly limit the system’s 
overall throughput but also significantly increase energy consumption and carbon 
emissions. This issue has become critically important under the increasingly stringent 
global policies on sustainable development and carbon neutrality: 
• Significant Carbon Footprint : Carbon emissions from transportation and 
warehousing acti

vities account for approximately 24% of total global greenhouse 
gas emissions and are considered a priority area for carbon reduction by 
international logistics leaders like DHL [2]. 

 
2 
• Urgent Policy Pressure : The European Union’s Carbon Border Adjustment 
Mechanism (CBAM), initiated in 2023 and set to take full effect in 2026, will 
impose costs on high -ca

rbon-intensity imported products. This will compel 
businesses throughout the supply chain to rigorously scrutinize and manage 
carbon emissions in their warehousing and transportation links [3]. 
 Against this backdrop, academia has begun applying intelligent methods like 
Reinforcement Learning (RL) to optimize RMFS operations. Some studies

have used 
deep reinforcement learning for order scheduling to minimize costs [4], while others have 
reduced total energy consumption by about 3–5% by adjusting traffic strategies and robot 
speeds [5]. However, these studies either focus on order -level scheduling or leave room 
for exploration in the trade -off between energy consumption and efficiency. More 
specifically, there is still a lack of a cont

rol framework focused at the intersection 
level that aims to achieve the dual optimization goals of “energy -efficiency” at a 
micro-level by reducing unnecessary waiting and frequent “stop-and-go” behaviors. 
 To fill this research gap, this study employs Neuroevolution Reinforcement 
Learning (NERL) to design an intersection controller. This method combines the global 
search capabilities of evolutionary algorithms with the real -time decision -making 
advantages of deep reinforcement learning. It aims to provide an “adaptive, energy-aware” 
right-of-way allocation mechanism for RMFS intersections, with the expectation of 
effectively reducing the energy consumption per task for AMRs while maintaining order 
processing efficiency. This is a direct technical approach to addressing the challenge of a 
high carbon footprint. 

 
3 
1.2 Research Objectives 
 The core objective of this study is to investigate and quantify the trade -off 
re

lationship between two conflicting goals —system throughput  and energy 
efficiency—for different control strategies addressing traffic congestion in Robotic 
Mobile Fulfillment Systems (RMFS). The specific objectives are threefold: 
1. Design and Implement an Adaptive Controller: Design and implement a traffic 
controller based on Neuroevolution Reinforcement Learning (NERL). The 
controller will utilize a global reward function aimed at balancing multiple 
macroscopic indicators, endowing it with the potential to autonomously learn  
complex trade-off strategies. 
2. Quantify the Performance of Different Control Philosophies : Systematically 
compare the comprehensive performance of three strategies representing different 
control philosophies—the proposed adaptive learning strategy (NERL), a static 
centralized strategy (Time -Based), and a decentralized strategy (No 
Control)—under a standardized simulation environment. 
3. Analyze and Elucidate Performance Trade -off Characteristics: Through in -
depth analysis of experimental data, elucidate the relative positioning and 
characteristics of the three strategies within the “throughput-energy” trade-off 
space. This study aims to answer: Can NERL find a better comprehensive benefit 
balance point compared to traditional, single-objective-oriented strategies? 
1.3 Research Scope and Assumptions 
 To ensure the depth and focus of this study, its research scope and corresponding 
assumptions are clearly defined as follows: 

 
4 
1.3.1 Research Scope 
• Problem Level: This study focuses on Intersection-level Traffic Control. The 
core of the research is to coordinate the right -of-way for robots at intersections, 
without involving Global Path Planning for the robots. 
• Experimental Scenario: All comparisons and analyses will be conducted in a 
standardized Medium-density warehouse scenario. This setting is designed to 
create a stable experimental environment that poses significant traffic challenges 
while avoiding system gridlock due to extreme congestion. 
• Evaluation Dimensions: Performance evaluation will adopt a Multi-objective 
Perspective, focusing on analyzing the performance of each controller on the two 
core metrics of system throughput and energy efficiency, a

nd their inherent trade-
off. 
• Comparative Philosophies: The core of this study is to compare three strategies 
representing different control philosophies (decentralized, static centralized, 
adaptive learning), aiming to deeply understand their intrinsic operational 
mechanisms and performance characteristics, rather than conducting a large-scale 
“audition” of algorithms. 
1.3.2 Core Assumptions for Focused Research 
To conduct an in-depth analysis of the intrinsic mechanisms of specific control strategies, 
this study is based on the following core assumptions to ensure consistency in comparison 
and depth of analysis: 
• Algorithm Breadth : To deeply analyze the potential of NERL in trade -off 
learning, this study will not conduct a horizontal performance comparison of 

 
5 
various deep reinforcement learning algorithms (such as DQN, A3C, etc.).  
• Scenario Generalizability: The conclusions of this study are based on a specific 
medium-density scenario. To ensure the depth of analysis, the performance of the 
controllers in higher or lower density scenarios, as well as their generalizability to 
different warehouse layo

uts, are assumed to be beyond the scope of this study. 
• Path Planning: This study assumes that the paths of all robots are provided by an 
independent, default path planning system (such as the A* algorithm). The traffic 
controller’s responsibility is solely to resolve local conflicts and does not include 
dynamically re-planning paths for robots. 
• Hyperparameter Optimization : This study will use a fixed set of 
hyperparameter configurations for the NERL controller and will not  conduct 
systematic Hyperparameter Tuning or Ablation Studies, to ensure all comparisons 
are made under a unified baseline. 
  

 
6 
CHAPTER 2  
LITERATURE REVIEW 
2.1 Chapter Overview 
 This chapter aims to systematically review the rese

arch related to traffic 
management in Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems (RMFS). It will unfold sequentially: 
first, by elaborating on the root causes of traffic congestion in RMFS and its profound 
impact on system performance; second, by evaluating three categories of control 
strategies relevant to this study —traditional heuristic methods, mainstream Deep 
Reinforcement Learning (DRL) methods, and emerging Neuroevolution methods —
assessing their applications, achievements, and limitations in solving this problem. 
Through this structured analysis, this chapter aims to pinpoint the existing research gap 
and ultimately clarify the necessity and novelty of propos ing a traffic control strategy 
based on Neuroevolution Reinforcement Learning (NERL) that integrates energy 
considerations. 
2.2 Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems: Operational 
Efficiency and Traffic Bottlenecks 
 RMFS, as a “Goods-to-Person” paradigm for warehouse automation, fundamentally 
replaces traditional manual picking routes with a large number of Autonomous Mobile 
Robots (AMRs) that transport mobile shelves (Pods), thereby significantly enhancing 
order ful fillment efficiency [6]. Since Kiva Systems was acquired by Amazo

n and 
deployed on a massive scale [7], RMFS has become a cornerstone of modern e-commerce 
logistics. Studies show that its productivity can be two to four times that of traditional 

 
7 
warehouses. As the number of robots deployed by companies like Amazon exceeds 
750,000, the operational density and complexity of these systems have reached 
unp

recedented levels [8]. 
 However, high -density deployment brings a new and severe bottleneck: Traffic 
Congestion. As defined in Chapter 3 of this study, when a large number of robots perform 
tasks within a limited aisle network, especially at intersections, conflicts and waiting 
periods easily arise from improper right-of-way coordination [9]. This triggers a series of 
cascading negative effects, not only causing delays in order fulfillment and reducing 
overall system throughput but also, as recent research indicates, the frequent stop-and-go 
behavior significant

ly increases overall energy consumption [10] and may accelerate 
robot battery aging, thereby increasing operational costs. Therefore, designing an 
intersection management strategy  that is adaptive and considers both efficiency and 
energy sustainability has become a core challenge in enhancing the overall performance 
of RMFS. 
 In the complex decision-making framework of RMFS, traffic control and path 
planning are the most critical elements related to real-time system performance. In recent 
years, much research has been dedicated to solving this problem. For example, one study 
proposed [11] proposed a path planning method combining the A* algorithm with DQN, 
while another study used [12] used an attention -based DRL model to collaboratively 
handle multi -AGV sche

duling and shelf reconfiguration. These studies highlight the 
immense potential of intelligent algorithms in RMFS operational optimization. Although 
this study focuses on intersection traffic control, its optimization goals —improving 
system fluidity and efficiency—are fundamentally aligned with these research efforts. 

 
8 
2.3 Review and Analysis of Intersection Tr

affic Control 
Methods 
 To address the problem of intersection traffic in RMFS, various control strategies 
have been proposed by academia and industry. Their development can be broadly 
categorized into heuristic methods and learning -based methods, reflecting a trend from 
static rules to dynamic, adaptive control. 
2.3.1 Heuristic and Rule-based Controllers 
 Early solutions often relied on manually designed heuristic rules. Their

advantages 
include simple implementation and low computational overhead, making them common 
baselines for evaluating the performance of more complex algorithms. 
 One category is Static Time-based Control, which borrows from traditional urban 
traffic signal systems by setting fixed cycles for switching right -of-way at intersections. 
T

his approach is conceptually similar to early rule-based urban traffic solutions [13] and 
corresponds to the TimeBasedController designed in Section 3.3.1 of this study. 
However, its fundamental flaw is its complete inability to adapt to real -time traffic flow 
changes. When traffic fluctuates dramatically, this static strategy is prone to wasting green 
light time o

n idle directions or causing unnecessary queue build-up in busy directions. 
 To overcome the shortcomings of static methods, another category, Dynamic 
Reactive Control, emerged. These methods react to the real-time state of the intersection 
(e.g., queue length). For instance,  the work in assigned agent  [14] assigned agents to 
intersections to

manage traffic flow based on the priority of incoming robots. Although 
more flexible, the design of these heuristic rules is highly dependent on expert experience 

 
9 
and prior knowledge. Their predefined weights and logic struggle to capture the optimal 
coordination strategy in complex traffic patterns, often leading to sub -optimal 
performance in highly dynamic and non-linear traffic scenarios [15]. 
2.3.2 Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)-based Controllers 
 To overcome the limitations of manual rules, Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) 
has been widely applied to traffic control problems. DRL enables an agent (the controller) 
to autonomously learn complex decision -making policies through interaction with the 
environment to maximize long-term cumulative rewards [16]. 
 This paradigm has achieved great success in the macroscopic field of urban traffic 
signal control. Numerous studies have demonstrated that methods based on Deep Q -
Networks (DQN) and its variants significantly outperform traditional methods in reducing 
vehicle delays and improving network throughput [17]. For example, Cao et al.

proposed 
[18] proposed G -DQN to improve convergence speed;  Zhu et al.  [19] compared the 
performance of PPO and DQN in single -intersection control; and Angela et al.  [20] 
designed a reward function that incorporates multi -dimensional information such as 
pressure, queue, and speed to train DQN agents. These studies fully demonstrate the 
powerful capabilities of DRL in handling sequential decision-making problems. 
 In recent years, researchers have begun to transfer this paradigm to RMFS scenarios. 
They typically use local traffic information at intersections (e.g., queue length, waiting 
time) as the State, switching traffic phases as the Action, and maximizing throughput or 
minimizing waiting time as the Reward to train the controller [21] [

22]. Although DQN-
based methods show great potential, they also face some inherent challenges:  
1. Reward Design Sensitivity: The performance of DQN is highly dependent on dense 

 
10 
and well-designed immediate reward signals. However, in complex trade-off problems 
like the one in this study, designing a perfect immediate reward is extremely difficult. 
A poorly designed or overly simplified reward function can lead the agent to learn 
unintended, sub-optimal behaviors (e.g., frequent signal flickering to gain switching 
rewards) as also noted in recent RL-based signal-control studies. [31] [33] 
2. Risk of Local Optima: In complex, high-dimensional state spaces, gradient-based 
optimization methods can sometimes converge to a local optimum, failing to discover 
the globally optimal coord

ination strategy. [34] 
3. Sample Efficiency Issues : DQN often requires a large number of environmental 
interactions to learn an effective policy, and the training process can be very time -
consuming. [35] 
These challenges, particularly the sensitivity of reward design and the risk of local optima, 
make it fundamentally difficult for DQN to handle the core “throughput -energy” multi-
objective tr

ade -off problem of this study. Designing a dense reward signal th at can 
accurately reflect long-term macroscopic goals in real-time is extremely complex and can 
easily lead the policy into sub -optimal behaviors. It is to address these issues that this 
study turns to a learning paradigm more suited to handling s

parse, de layed, and global 
rewards. These limitations have motivated scalable/federated variants of DQN and 
gradient-free EvoRL alternatives better suited to sparse or delayed rewards [31] [32] [33]. 
2.4 The Potential of Neuroevolution Reinforcement 
Learning (NERL) 
 To address the challenges faced by traditional DRL methods, this study introduces 

 
11 
another optimization paradi

gm —Neuroevolution Reinforcement Learning (NERL). 
Unlike DQN, which attempts to learn a value function, NERL searches directly in the 
parameter space of the policy network. It treats the weights of a neural network as an 
individual’s “genes” and uses evolutionary algorithms (such as elitism, crossover, and 
mutation) to optimize an entire population of agents, ultimately yielding a high -
performance control policy [23]. 
 NERL exhibits several unique advantages in solving complex control problems, 
making it

particularly suitable for the objectives of this study:  
1. Robustness to Reward Functions : Evolutionary algorithms are gradient -free, 
black-box optimization methods that do not require the reward function to be 
continuous or differentiable. This makes them naturally applicable to handling sparse, 
delayed, or global performance -based reward signals . For example, the “total 
number of completed orders” or “total energy consumption” over an entire simulation 
episode can be directly used as the fitness function. This aligns well with the Global 
Reward concept designed in Section 3.4.4 of this study, thus avoiding tedious Reward 
Engineering.  
2.

Stronger Global Exploration: The crossover and mutation operations in the 
evolutionary process facilitate broader exploration in the parameter space. By 
maintaining population diversity, the risk of getting stuck in local optima is reduced, 
making it more likely to discover innovative and efficient control strategies.  
3. Inherent Parallelism: The evaluation of each individual (policy network) in the 
population is independent and can be deployed in parallel on multi -core CPUs or 
computing clusters. This allows for effective use of modern computing resources, 

 
12 
significantly reducing the wall-clock time for train

ing [24]. 
 Although neuroevolution has achieved remarkable success in fields like robotics 
control and game AI [25], the specific application of it to RMFS intersection traffic 
control, and a systematic comparison with traditional heuristic methods and mainstream 
DRL methods (like DQN) on a comprehensive platform that considers both time 
efficiency and energy consumption, remains a research gap. 
2.5 Chapter Summary and Research Gap 
 Based on the analysis above, the development trajectory of existing RMFS traffic 
control research clearly points to several k

ey unresolved issues. Control strategies are 
evolving from simple static rules to dynamic heuristics, and then to DRL-based adaptive 
methods. However, existing DRL methods (mainly DQN) still face challenges in reward 
design, avoiding local optima, and optimizing for lo

ng-term global objectives. Meanwhile, 
most traffic control research focuses on throughput and time delay, with a relative scarcity 
of studies that treat energy consumption  as a core optimization target on par with 
efficiency. 
 More importantly, the potential of neuroevolution, as a method with stronger global 
exploration capabilities and greater robustness to sparse global rewards, has not been 
systematically explored and validated for the RMFS traffic control problem. 
 Therefore, the core research gap of this study can be summarized as: a lack of an 
intelligent control strategy for RMFS that can effectively balance and optimize traffic 
efficiency and energy consumption, while overcoming the limitations of traditional DRL 
in areas such as reward design and local optima. 

 
13 
 To fill this gap, this paper proposes an intersection controller based on 
Neuroevolution Reinforcement Learning (NERL). We will conduct a comprehensive 
comparison of NERL with traditional heuristic methods and a standard DQN method on 
a high-fidelity simulation platform that incorporates a detailed energy consumption model. 
This research not only aims to propose a superior control strategy but also provides a solid 
empirical basis for evaluating the merits of different learning paradigms in solving 
complex multi-agent coordination problems. 
 To more intuitively compare this study with related literature, Table 2.1 summarizes 
the similarities and differences between selected representative studies and this research 
in terms of problem definition, decision criteria, and algorithmic appr

CHAPTER 3  
METHODOLOGY 
3.1 Problem Definition 
 In modern automated warehouses utilizing Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems 
(RMFS), traffic congestion has become a core bottleneck constraining overall operational 
efficiency as order volumes and robot deployment densities increase. A large number of 
robots executing tasks within a limited aisle ne

twork , especially at intersections, can 
easily lead to conflicts and waiting due to improper right -of-way coordination. This 
triggers a series of cascading negative effects, including delays in order fulfillment, 
energy waste from robots waiting idly, and a decline in the system’s overall throughput. 
 Therefore, the central problem of this research is: How to design an effective and 
self-adaptive traffic control strategy to dynamically regulate right-of-way at intersections, 
thereby minimizing robot

waiting times and overall energy consumption, and ult imately 
enhancing the operational efficiency of the entire warehouse system? 
 To answer this question, it is essential to first construct an experimental platform that 
can accurately simulate the aforementioned challenges. The experimental environment 
and system architecture described in this chapter constitute a high-fidelity testbed tailored 
for this core problem. Its purpose is to provide a stable, controllable, and quantifiable 
foundation for the implementation, training, and comparison of different control 
algorithms in subsequent chapters. 

 
16 
3.1.1 Chapter Structure 
 This section aims to provide a comprehensive introduction to the experimental 
platform supporting this research. The content will cover four core parts: first, a detailed 
description of the constructed warehouse simulation environment, including its physi cal 
layout and core entities (Section 3.2.1); second, an explanation of the traffic control 
system architecture designed for algorithm evaluation (Section 3.2.2); third, a list of the 
specific hardware and software configurations used in the experiments (S ection 3.2.3); 
and finally, the definition of the quantitative evaluation metrics used to measure the 
effectiveness of each control strategy (Section 3.2.4). Through this section, readers will 
gain a complete understanding of the foundational environment a nd evaluation criteria 
for the experiments. 
3.2 Experimental Environment and System 
Architecture 
 To effectively validate and compare the Neuroevolution Reinforcement Learning 
(NERL) traffic control strategy proposed in this study with other baseline methods, it is 
necessary to construct a high -fidelity simulation platform that accurately reflects the  
challenges of real-world warehouse operations. This platform is not only a testing ground 
for algorithms but also a prerequisite for ensuring that all comparisons are conducted on 
a fair, controllable, and quantifiable basis. 
 This section will comprehensively describe the composition of this experimental 
platform from the inside out, from foundation to evaluation:  
 Simulation World Construction: First, we will detail the two foundations of the 

 
17 
simulation world—the physical environment design of the warehouse (Section 3.2.1)  
and the physics and energy consumption model (Section 3.2.5) that endows the robots 
with realistic energy characteristics. These two elements together define the “physical 
laws” of the experiment.  
 Control System

Design : Next, we introduce the traffic control system 
architecture (Section 3.2.2) designed for flexible algorithm integration, which serves as 
the “central nervous system” of the experiment.  
 Evaluation Framework and Environment : Finally, to ensure the objectivity and 
reproducibility of the research, we will clearly define the performance evaluation 
metrics (Section 3.2.4)  used to measure effectiveness and list the hardware and 
software configurations (Section 3.2.3) used in the experiments. 
Through this section, readers will gain a complete understanding of the experimental 
environment, control logic, and evaluation standards of this study. 
3.2.1 Warehouse Simulation Environment Design 
 To effectively evaluate traffic control strategies, this study first constructed a high -
fidelity warehouse simulation environment. This environment not only defines the 
physical layout but also includes various dynamic entities and their interaction rules , 
which together form a complex Robotic Mobile Fulfillment System (RMFS). This section 
will detail its design. 
1. Physical Environment and Layout 
The simulated warehouse is built on a two -dimensional discretized grid, with each grid 
cell having a specific function. The overall layout adopts a functional zoning design to 

 
18 
ensure an orderly operational flow. 
• Central Storage Area: Located in the center of the warehouse, it consists of a 
dense arrangement of Pod Locations. The aisles in this area are designed as strict 
One-way Aisles , with the flow directions of horizontal and vertical aisles 
alternating. This design greatly simplifies traffic management complexity at the 
physical level, aiming to reduce potential conflicts from robots moving in opp

• Workstation Area: Distributed on both sides of the warehouse. One side houses 
Picking Stations, which are the exit points for order fulfillment, and the other 
side has Replenishment Stations, the entry points for goods into the system. 
• Charging Stations: Scattered within the storage area, converted from some pod 
locations, for robots to charge autonomousl
 
Figure 3.2.1 simulated warehou

se 


 
19 
2. Core Entities and Lifecycles 
 The dynamic behavior of the system is driven by the interactions among several core 
entities. 
• Robot: As the most central active unit in the system, the robot has a complex state 
machine to manage its workflow, including states like idle, taking_pod, 
delivering_pod, station_processing, and returning_pod. This study establishes a 
detailed physics and ener gy model for the robot. Its energy consumption 
calculation considers not only the load but also startup costs and regenerative 
braking (energy recovery during braking), providing a solid basis for energy 
efficiency evaluation. Additionally, robots hav e a priority -based autonomous 
obstacle avoidance logic, enabling them to resolve l

ocal conflicts to some extent.
 
Figure 3.2.2 Robot Lifecycle 
• Pod: The mobile carrier for storing goods (SKUs). Each pod can hold multiple 
types of SKUs and records the current quantity and replenishment threshold for 
each SKU. When the stock level falls below the threshold, the system 
automatically trigg

ers a corresponding replenishment task. 


 
20 
• Station: The node for human-robot collaboration. When a robot delivers a pod to 
a station, the system simulates the delay of a worker picking or replenishing goods. 
To handle high traffic, stations are also designed with a dynamic path adjustment 
mechanism, activating a longer backup path to alleviate congestion when there 
are too many robots at the station. 
3. Order and Task Flow 
 The driving force of the simulation comes from orde

rs. An Order represents a 
customer’s request and contains multiple SKUs to be picked. The system breaks down an 
order into one or more Jobs. The core of a job is “transporting a specified pod to a 
specified workstation,” which is the smallest work unit that can be directly assigned to a 
robot.  
The entire process is as follows:  
1. The system receives an order.  
2. The SKUs required for the order are located on specific pods.  
3. The system generates one or more jobs and places them in the job queue.  
4. An idle robot takes a job from the queue and begins its work cycle of 
picking up, delivering, and returning the pod.  
5. When all SKUs for an order have been successfully delivered to the 
workstation, the order is marked as complete. 
4. Intersection Design and Classification 
 In addition to the macroscopic layout, this study provides a clear classification and 
definition of the microscopic traffic nodes within the warehouse—the intersections. This 
is crucial for the subsequent controller d

esign. 

 
21 
• Standard Intersection : This is the basic unit that constitutes the main traffic 
network of the warehouse, formed by the convergence of two mutually 
perpendicular one-way aisles. All intersections not otherwise specified fall into 
this category. 
• Critical Intersection : Based on their strategic importance in the warehouse 
layout, some intersections are marked as “critical.” These are traffic nodes on the 
entry or exit paths directly connected to workstations (picking or repl

enishment 
stations). They are the mandatory routes for entering and exiting the workstations 
and constitute the primary bottlenecks of the entire warehouse system. The 
efficiency of managing these intersections will directly affect workstation 
throughput and robot queue lengths, a nd may trigger spillback traffic into the 
storage area. Therefore, in the design of the reinforcement learning rewar

d 
function (see Section 3.4. 4), these intersections will be given higher weights to 
guide the agent to prioritize their effective management

3.2.2 Traffic Control System Architecture 
 To enable flexible integration and fair comparison of different traffic control 
algorithms, this study designed a modular software architecture based on the Strategy 
Pattern and Factory Pattern. The core of this architecture is to separate the “decision -
making algorithm” from the “system execution framework,” ensuring that whether it’s a 
simple rule-based logic or a complex deep reinforcement learning model, it can operate 
and be evaluated on th e same foundation. The system is mainly composed of the 
following three components: 
1. TrafficController (Abstract Base Class for Traffic Controllers) 
 This Abstract Base Class (ABC) defines a unified interface that all traffic control 
strategies must adhere to. Its core method is get_direction(intersection, tick, warehouse), 
which receives the current detailed state of the intersection (local informatio n) and the 


 
23 
state of the entire warehouse system (global information), and returns a traffic decision 
for that intersection (e.g., "Horizontal" or "Vertical"). By forcing all controllers to 
implement this interface, the system ensures consistency in how different algorithms are 
called. Additionally, the base class integrates standardized statistical data collection 
functions to record various performance metrics. 
2.

TrafficControllerFactory (Controller Factory) 
 This class uses the factory design pattern and is responsible for dynamically creating 
instances of TrafficController subclasses based on external configurations (such as the 
controller type specified in the experiment configuration file). When the simulation core 
needs a controller, it only needs to provide a string identifier like "dqn", "nerl", or 
"time_based", and the factory will return a corresponding, initialized controller object. 
This design completely decouples the “creation logic” from the “usag e logic” of the 
controllers, greatly enhancing the flexibility and scalability of the experimental process, 
allowing for switching between different control strategies without modifying any core 
simulation code. 
3. IntersectionManager (Intersection Manager) 
 The IntersectionManager is the central coordinator and execution engine of the 
entire traffic control system. Its operational flow forms a complete closed -loop control 
system:  
1. Holds Controller Instance : During the simulation initialization phase, the 
IntersectionManager obtains the required controller instance for the current experiment 
through the controller factory.  
2. Drives Decision Loop : In each time unit (tick) of the simulation, the manager 

 
24 
iterates through all intersections in the warehouse.  
3. Gets Decision : For each intersection, it calls the get_direction() method of the 
TrafficController instance to get the traffic command for that intersection.  
4. Executes Decision : Based on the command returned by the controller, the 
IntersectionManager updates the internal state of the intersection, such as changing the 
allowed direction of passage.  
5.

Triggers Model Training: Specifically, for reinforcement learning-based controllers 
(DQN/NERL), after the decision and execution steps are completed, the 
IntersectionManager will then call its train() method, providing the model with the just-
occurred state transition (State -Action-Reward-NextState), enabling it to learn and 
optimize from experience.

3.3 Baseline Control Strategies 
 To objectively evaluate the performance of the Neuroevolution Reinforcement 
Learning (NERL) traffic control method proposed in this study, it must be compared 
against a set of representative Baseline Strategies. These baseline strategies provide a 
performance reference point, allowing us to quantify the actual improvements brought by 
the complex algorithm. This study selects two distinct control philosophies as baselines: 
a Time-Based Strategy representing traditional static centralized management, and a No-
Control Strategy repres

enting fully decentralized decision-making. 
3.3.1  Time-Based Controller 
 The TimeBasedController is the most basic static traffic control strategy. Its core 
idea is derived from traditional urban traffic signal systems, completely ignoring real -
time traffic flow or any dynamic changes at the intersection. It relies solely on a  pre-set, 
fixed time cycle to switch the right -of-way between horizontal and vertical directions. 
The advantage of this method is its extreme simplicity and predictability, but its 
disadvantage is equally obvious—it cannot adapt to fluctuations in traffic demand, easily 
causing unnecessary waiting during busy times or wasting passage time during light 
traffic. 
Design Principles and Decision Logic 
 The operation of this controller is determined entirely by two core parameters: the 
green light time for the horizontal direction ( 𝑇𝐻_𝑔𝑟𝑒𝑒𝑛) and the green light time for the 
vertical direction ( 𝑇𝑉_𝑔𝑟𝑒𝑒𝑛). The sum

of these two constitutes a complete signal cycle 
length (𝑇𝑐𝑦𝑐𝑙𝑒). 

 
34 
𝑇𝑐𝑦𝑐𝑙𝑒 = 𝑇𝐻_𝑔𝑟𝑒𝑒𝑛 + 𝑇𝑉_𝑔𝑟𝑒𝑒𝑛  (3 − 11) 
 At any given time tick in the simulation, the controller determines the current 
position in the signal cycle (𝑡𝑝𝑜𝑠) using a modulo operation: 
𝑡𝑝𝑜𝑠 = tick mod 𝑇𝑐𝑦𝑐𝑙𝑒  (3 − 12) 
 Based on the value of 𝑡𝑝𝑜𝑠, the controller decides the direction of passage. If 𝑡𝑝𝑜𝑠 
is less than the horizontal green time 𝑇𝐻_𝑔𝑟𝑒𝑒𝑛, it grants the right-of-way to the horizontal 
direction; otherwise, it grants it to the vertical direction.

3.3.2 Priority-Based Decentralized Strategy 
 To establish a performance baseline, this study designed a priority -based 
decentralized strategy. This strategy can also be viewed as a “No -Central-Controller” 
scenario, intended to simulate an environment without any central traffic coordination 
mechanism. In this setting, the right-of-way at intersections is determined entirely by the 
robots themselves based on a simple, predefined set of priority rules. This strategy 
represen

ts an important performance floor, used to measure the net benefit of any 
centralized control measure. 
Design Principles and Decision Logic 
 When multiple robots have a potential conflict at an intersection, the system assigns 
a priority to each robot based on its current task state. The priority levels are defined in

3.4 Neuroevolution Reinforcement Learning (NERL) 
Controller Design 
 To apply the Neuroevolution Reinforcement Learning framework to the traffic 
control problem of this study, we first establish the mapping of its core elements, which 
are detailed in the following subsections:  
 Agent:

The traffic controller at each intersection.  
 Environment: The high-fidelity warehouse simulation system  as described 
in Section 3.2.1. 

 
36 
 State: The feature vector observed by the agent at the time of decision, its 
detailed composition is in Section 3.4.2.  
 Action: The discrete commands the agent can execute, its detailed composition 
is in Section 3.4.

3.  
 Reward: a global fitness score  calculated only at the end of an evaluation 
episode, its design philosophy and formula are detailed in Section 3.4.4. 
 To cope with the high dynamics and complexity of traffic within RMFS and to learn 
to trade -off between multiple conflicting objectives like “throughput” and “energy 
consumption,” this study employs Neuroevolution Reinforcement Learning (NERL)  
to develop an intell

igent traffic controller. 
 Unlike traditional gradient -based reinforcement learning methods (like DQN), 
NERL optimizes directly in the parameter space of the policy. In this framework, the 
weights and biases 𝜃 of a neural network controller (i.e., a policy 𝜋𝜃) are treated as an 
individual’s Genotype. The algorithm searches for the policy network parameters 𝜃∗ 
that maximize the system’s long -term macroscopic benefits by performing “evaluation -
selection-reproduction” evolutionary operations

on a Population of many individuals. 
This method is more robust to sparse, delayed global reward signals and is naturally suited 
for large-scale parallel training [25] [29]. 
 This section will detail the core evolutionary process of the NERL controller, the 
state space, the action space, and the crucial design of the global reward function. 
3.4.1 NERL Evolutionary Process 
 The core process of NERL revolves around an “evalua

. Assume at generation 𝑔  there is a population 𝑃𝑔 = {𝜃1, 𝜃2, . . . , 𝜃𝑁} , 
containing 𝑁 individuals. 
1. Evaluation: Each individual 𝜃𝑖 in the population is deployed in an independent 
simulation environment instance to run a full evaluation episode. During the 
episode, the policy 𝜋𝜃𝑖 makes all decisions independently. After the episode ends, 
a Fitness Score  𝐹(𝜃𝑖)  is calculated for the individual based on the system’s 
macroscopic performance metrics (detailed in Section 3.4.4). 
2.

Selection: Based on the calculated fitness scores of all individuals, the algorithm 
selects high-performing individuals from the current population 𝑃𝑔 as Parents to 
provide genes for the next generation. This study uses Tournament Selection. 
3.

Reproduction: A new offspring population 𝑃𝑔+1  is generated by applying 
genetic operations to the parents. The main operations include: 
o Elitism: To prevent losing the best solutions found during evolution, the 
top 𝑘  elite individuals with the highest fitness in each generation are 
directly and completely copied to the next generation’s population. 
o Crossover: Simulating biological reproduction, two individuals are 
selected from the parents, and their parameter vectors are mixed to create 
new offspring. 
o Mutation: A small random perturbation is applied to the parameters of the 
offspring individuals to maintain population diversity and avoid premature 
convergence. 
 By iteratively executing the above cycle, the average fitness of the population will 
gradually increase, eventually converging to a high-performance policy network that can 
efficiently solve complex traffic control problems. 

 
38

3.4.3 Action Space Design 
 This study designs a concise discrete action space 𝒜 for the DRL agent. At each

3.4.4 Global Reward Function Design 
 The reward function (often called the fitness function in evolutionary algorithms) is 
the core element that guides the agent’s behavior. To guide the NERL controller to learn 
complex trade-off strategies that are beneficial to the system’s long -term, macroscopic 
goals, this study designed a comprehensive Global Reward Function. In this model, the 
agent receives no immediate feedback throughout the evaluation episode. A single fitness 
score is calculated only at the end of the episode, based on the final ov erall performance 
of the system. 

 
41 
 The design of this function incorporates established principles from the field of 
multi-objective optimization [30] and includes customized penalty terms (such as a long-
tail delay penalty) tailored to the unique challenges of the RMFS scenario in this study, 
to more comprehensively capture the system’

s overall performance. Its core formula is 
defined as follows: 
Fitness = (𝑁orders ⋅ 𝑤orders) − (𝐸total ⋅ 𝑤energy) − ∑ (
𝑖∈𝑃
𝜆𝑖 ⋅ Penalty𝑖)  (3 − 14) 
where 𝑁orders  is the total number of completed orders, 𝐸total  is the total energy 
consumption, and 𝑤orders and 𝑤energy are their corresponding weights. ∑(𝜆𝑖 ⋅ Penalty𝑖) 
is a series of weighted penalty terms used to penalize undesirable system behaviors. 
Specifically, the penalty term 𝑃 includes the following key parts: 
• Maximum Wait Time Penalty (𝑃wait): Penalizes the maximum robot waiting time 
that occurred throughout the simulation to encourage fairness and prevent 
individual robots from starving. 
• Average Wait Time Penalty (𝑃avg wait): Penalizes the average time of all waiting 
events to improve overall traffic fluidity. 
• Stop-and-Go Penalty (𝑃sg): Penalizes the total number of stop -and-go events in 
the system to encourage smooth traffic flow and indirectly reduce energy 
consumption. 
• Underutilization Penalty ( 𝑃util ): Penalizes situations where the average robot 
utilization falls below a certain reference value to encourage the system to 
maintain high operational efficiency. 
• Long-Tail Delay Penalty ( 𝑃tail ): Penalizes high -percentile waiting times (e.g., 
P95/P99) to specifically address and improve the worst experiences in the system. 

 
42 
 All penalty terms are normalized based on a preset threshold before calculation, 
constraining them to the [0, 1] interval to ensure that penalties of different scales can be 
stably combined. This multi -objective, penalty -normalization-based fitness function 
design aims to guide the evolutionary process to find a comprehensively optimal solution 
that is not only high-throughput but also energy-efficient and highly stable. 
 It is important to emphasize that the effectiveness of this reward function is not 
proven through theoretical derivation but will be empirically validated by the 
experimental results in Chapter 4. If the NERL controller trained based on this function 
can demonstrate the expected intelligent trade-off behavior between multiple conflicting 
objectives in the final performance evaluation, then this design can be considered 
successful. 
3.5 Experimental Design and Evaluation Process 
 To rigorously answer the research questions posed in the introduction, this study 
designed a systematic three -phase experimental process. This process is intended to 
ensure that all control strategies are compared on a fair and controllable basis, and to 
clearly reveal the performance differences between different control philosophies. 
3.5.1 Overview of the Experimental Process 
The overall validation process follows these three core steps: 
1. Phase 1: Baseline Scenario Establishment  First, a series of preliminary 
simulations are conducted to determine a standard robot density  that can 
generate representative traffic pressure. This density will serve as a uniform 
configuration for all subsequent formal experiments to ensure consistency in 

 
43 
comparison. 
2. Phase 2: Baseline Controller Optimization  Next, a systematic parameter 
sensitivity analysis is performed on the traditional Time-Based Controller. This 
step aims to find an optimal set of traffic light cycle parameters for it, to ensure 
that the subsequent comparison with NERL is made against a well -optimized 
baseline. 
3.

Phase 3: Final Performance Comparison  Finally, under the standardized 
experimental scenario, a comprehensive, multi -dimensional performance 
evaluation and comparison is conducted on the three strategies representing 
different control philosophies—NERL, the optimized Time-Based Controller, 
and the decentralized (no-control) strategy. 
3.5.2 Phase 1: Baseline Scenario Establishment 
 To establish a standardized testbed that can effectively evaluate controller 
performance, the core objective of this phase is to determine a “medium-pressure” robot 
density. If the density is too low, it will not generate enough traffic conflicts to distinguish 
between the merits of the controllers; if it is too high, the system may quickly become 
saturated under any control strategy, also preventing effective comparison. 
• Experimental Method: 
o The decentralized (no-control) strategy is used as the testing baseline. 
o The number of robots in the simulation is systematically varied, for 
example, starting from 20 and increasing in increments of 5 up to 40. 
o For each density setting, 20 complete, long -duration simulations of 
100,000 ti

cks are run repeatedly. 

 
44 
• Evaluation Metrics: 
o The main observation metrics are total system throughput (number of 
completed orders) and average energy consumption per order. 
• Decision Criteria: 
o The number of robots at which significant performance bottlenecks begin 
to appear (e.g., throughput growth slows while energy consumption 
continues to rise) is selected as the standard configura

tion for all 
subsequent experiments. 
3.5.3 Phase 2: Baseline Controller Optimization 
 To ensure a fair comparison with the baseline, this phase aims to find the optimal 
parameter configuration for the Time-Based Controller. 
• Experimental Method: 
o Experiments are conducted at the standard robot density determined in 
Phase 1. 
o A systematic parameter sweep is performed on the two core parameters of 
the time-based controller—horizontal green time and vertical green time. 
For example, while keeping the total cycle length constant (e.g., 100 ticks), 
different time allocation ratios (e.g., 90/10, 80/20, …, 20/80) are tested. 
o Each parameter combination is run multiple times in simulation.

• Evaluation Metrics: 
o The main observation metrics are order completion rate and total energy 
consumption.

3.6 NERL Training Process and Parameter Settings 
 To ensure the reproducibility of the research results, this section details the training 
process and key hyperparameters used for the NERL controller. The parameter 
configuration listed here was determined based on a series of preliminary experiments on 
convergence and stability. This study does not claim this configuration to be the global 
optimum but presents it as a standardized setup that can stably train high -performance 
models and r

eliably reproduce the core findings of this paper. 
3.6.1 Training Process 
 The training of NERL is a generation-iterative, off-policy learning process. A single 
complete training experiment follows this procedure: 
1. Initialization: 

 
47 
a. An instance of NEController is created according to the hyperparameters 
described in Section 3.6.2. 
b. The controller randomly initializes a population of 30 network individuals. 
2.

Evolutionary Loop: 
a. An evolution is started for 20 generations. 
b. In each generation g: 
i. Parallel Evaluation: For the 30 individuals in the population, 30 
independent simulation environments are launched. 
ii. Each individual runs a full evaluation episode of 25,000 ticks in its 
dedicated environment. To ensure consistency in learning 
objectives, all evaluations are conducted under a fixed order 
demand; however, a different random seed is used for each 
evaluation to change initial conditions (like Pod layout), thereby 
generating diverse, dynamic training scenarios for the controller 
through real-time interaction with the simulator. 
iii.

After the episode, the fitness score of the individual is calculated 
according to the global reward function defined in Section 3.4.4. 
iv. Evolutionary Operation : Once the fitness scores of all 
individuals are calculated, the controller performs a full 
evolutionary operation (selection, crossover, mutation) to generate 
a new offspring population. 
3. Model Saving: 
a. At the end of each generation, the algorithm saves the individual with the 
highest fitness in that generation as the best model of that generation. 
b. After the evolution of all generations is complete, the model with the 

 
48 
highest fitness score across all generations is selected as the final model 
for the final performance comparison in Section 3.5.4.

5.1 Research Summary 
 This study aimed to investigate the trade -off relationship between “system 
throughput” and “energy efficiency” for different traffic control philosophies in 
addressing traffic congestion in Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems (RMFS). To this end, 
this paper first established a standard

ized, medium -density warehouse simulation 
scenario. On this basis, a s ystematic comparative analysis was conducted on three 
representative strategies: adaptive learning (NERL), static centralized (Time-Based), and 
priority-based decentralized. 
 According to the experimental results in Chapter 4, this study revealed a clear 
performance trade-off among the different strategies:  
 The priority-based decentralized strategy  established the upper limit for system 
throughput, with an order completion rate significantly higher than the other two 
controlled strategies. However, this high output came at the cost of substantial energy 
consumption, making it representative of a “high-throughput, high-energy” approach.

 The optimized Time-Based strategy’s performance on various metrics

Overall, the core finding of this

cost (energy) savings with a manageable trade-off in capacity.

framework for the RMFS traffic control problem that focuses on the “throughput-energy” 
trade-off analysis, and validating the unique potential of Neuroevolution Reinforcement

71 
• Limitation in Algorithm Breadth: The core of this study was to compare three 
different control “philosophies,” not to conduct a large -scale performance 
competition of algorithms. Therefore, to deeply analyze the potential of NERL in 
trade-off learning, the research scope did not include  a horizontal performance 
comparison of various deep reinforcement learning

algorithms (such as DQN, 
A3C, etc.). 
• Limitation in Scenario Generalizability: All conclusions of this study are based 
on a specific, medium -density warehouse scenario. Although the scenario was 
designed to be representative, whether the findings of this study, especially 
regarding the performance ranking and energy -saving potentia l of different 
strategies, can be directly generalized to higher or lower density, or to warehouse 
environments with different physical layouts, remains an open question that 
requires further empirical research. 
• Independence of Path Planning: This study assumed that the global paths of all 
robots were pre-determined by an independent A* algorithm. The core focus was 
on local conflict coordination at the intersection level and did not involve the 
possibility of jointly o

ptimizing traffic control with global path planning. 
• Fixity of Hyperparameters : To ensure consistency in comparison, this study 
used a fixed set of hyperparameter configurations for the NERL controller. The 
study did not conduct systematic hyperparameter tuning or ablation studies, so it 
cannot be claimed that the current configuration is the global optimum. 
5.4 Future Work 
Based on the findings and existing limitations of this study, future research ca

n be 
deepened and expanded in the following directions: 

 
72 
1. Expanding Training Scale to Explore Performance Limits : Although the 
performance trend line of the NERL training in this study showed an upward trend 
after 20 generations, it had not yet reached a fully converged stable state. 
Therefore, a direct and valuable future research direction is to significantly 
extend the number of training generations (e.g., to 50 or 100 generations) with 
more powerful computational resources. This would help to more fully explore 
the upper potential of the NERL model and could verify whether longer training 
can lead the agent to find a more advantageous Pareto Optimal  front [30] in the 
“throughput-energy” trade-off space. 
2. Cross-Scenario Generalization Ability Verification: To more comprehensively 
evaluate the robustness of the NERL controller, future research could deploy the 
optimal model trained in this study directly into warehouse environments with 
different robot densities (higher or lower) and different physical layouts for “Zero-
shot” or “Fine -tuning” tests. This would allow for a deeper evaluation of its 
policy’s generalization ability and reveal potential weaknesses of the current 
model in adapting to new environments. 
3. Joint Optimization of Traffic Control and Global Path Planning : This study 
treated path planning and traffic control as two separate modules. However, in the 
real world, the two are highly coupled. Future research could explore a more 
ambitious framework, for example, by feeding the congestion level of 
intersections as a dynamic cost back to the upstream global path planner, thereby 
constructing a multi-agent learning system that can achieve “path planning-traffic 
control” joint optimization. 
4.

Systematic Hyperparameter and Reward Function Ablation Studies : This 
study used a fixed set of hyperparameters and a comprehensive global reward 

 
73 
function. Future research could conduct more detailed ablation studies, for 
example, by systematically analyzing the impact of key hyperparameters like 
population size and mutation rate on learning efficiency and final performance; or 
by conducting step-by-step removal tests of the various penalty terms in the global 
reward function (such as waiting penalty, stop -and-go penalty, etc.) to quantify 
the specific contribution of each component in guiding the agent to learn specific 
trade-off behaviors.
